questions.text: I find it hard to tell when someone is deferring to me out of genuine respect versus obligation.
I believe my emotional steadiness is a genuine trait, not a mask I wear.
I can feel the residue of a stressful phone call still buzzing in my body an hour later.
I am drawn to moments where a group's collective effort changes everything.
I detect the difference between someone sharing personal information to build trust and someone doing it to create a sense of obligation.
I routinely deplete my own reserves to make sure others are taken care of.
I feel that the moral demand of suffering is the standard by which I measure the authenticity of my own ethical commitments.
I notice that music, films, and stories move me intellectually more than they move me physically.
I believe that mastery comes from repeating the fundamentals until they are effortless.
I do not usually notice when someone is carefully not mentioning a person or topic that is obviously relevant.
A single harsh word can send a tremor through my entire nervous system.
I am the person who reframes a problem so the team can act on it.
I find that stepping back from the emotional content of a conversation about suffering allows me to hear more clearly what is actually being said.
I think that emotional sensitivity to others' pain, while admirable, can become a liability if not managed.
I focus on the conversation during meetings and trust the key points will stick.
My whole body responds to emotional shifts — posture, muscle tension, even the way I breathe.
I find it natural to ask others what they expect from themselves.
I prefer to own the outcome of a decision rather than share that ownership.
I believe that calm, evidence-based assessment of a difficult human situation is more likely to produce just outcomes than emotional engagement.
I routinely eat last, leave last, and take the smallest portion.
I tend to see my career advancement as one of my highest priorities.
I pick up on subtle vocal changes that signal someone is uncomfortable.
I am the person who usually says, 'Here is what we are going to do.'
I find that I am unapologetically focused on building the life I envision for myself.
I rarely notice when someone is performing a social role rather than being authentic.
I routinely make decisions based on what will best serve my personal trajectory.
I feel that the moral urgency of suffering calls for a response that is both principled and sustained, not impulsive or short-lived.
I feel most useful when I am steadying the group under pressure.
I follow up on requests I've made to ensure nothing falls through the cracks.
I feel that the moral demand of suffering is the force that has most decisively prevented me from living a life of comfortable moral passivity.
I notice when someone's entire demeanor shifts depending on whether their supervisor is present.
I rotate seasonal items in and out of storage on a predictable schedule.
I believe that bold strokes and big moves matter more than getting every detail right.
I avoid detailed project timelines because they rarely survive contact with reality.
I find that my appetite, sleep, and energy are rarely affected by emotional events.
I feel that my personal comfort matters less than the comfort of those around me.
